Currently, the interpolator assumes that intervals are disjoint and cover some range `[t_min, t_max]`; the fill value gets applied outside the interval.

Disjointness is required for the labeling process to be a function, so I just made that requirement explicit in the docstring.

Yes, deserves a comment. The issue is that we now have to account for the future being resolved in a different thread. Prior to this change we would block on network IO and then check the future's completion after processing the network IO and any corresponding futures. But now that we have separated future handling from network IO, and we have (intentionally) moved the future handling into a separate section that is not locked, we can have a situation where one thread is called with a simple timeout and another is called with a future (block until resolved). The first thread acquires the lock and receives the response that will resolve the future in its _poll() call. The response is put on the internal pending_completions queue and the lock is released. Now the second thread takes the lock and checks whether the future is resolved. It is not, so it calls _poll with a full request timeout, which is now 5 minutes. The first thread continues, without the lock, and begins processing pending_completions. The future is resolved with the queued response, and the first thread finishes. But the second thread is now waiting for network IO that it thinks is necessary to complete its future. Except that the future is already done and so there is no network IO coming. It will simply wait for 5 minutes and then timeout before rechecking the future and then finally returning.

My solution here is to simply reduce the network IO timeout when called with a future to reduce the unneeded block time when this occurs. Another solution might be to try to register any future that is "blocking" and call _wake_up() on when such a future is resolved. I decided against that approach because I think it is too complex and it is likely to add overhead to every response, not just ones for which some thread is blocking on a future.

It adds complexity (for users), but I think it's justified since real-world scheduling is complex, and whoever uses luigi in a big setting should actively think about the corner cases. Keep in mind that:

 * People might already rely on the current behaviour (exit code 1 on unhandled exceptions).
 * You want different return codes in different contexts.
   * Sometimes you simply want the "run until you succeed" behaviour that is the default (exit code 0 for failures/missingdata/alreadyrunning). 
   * Sometimes you want to run a command line, and feel confident that if I get exit code 0, my root task is in fact complete (then you would configure all exit codes).
   * If you use Jenkins as a cron-invoker, you would like task_failed to have a dedicated error code, causing "red" in Jenkins. Meanwhile already running or missing data exit codes should indicate "pending", or indicating your Jenkins dependencies mismatch what's configured in luigi.

That said. I would be thrilled to see this being simplified (convention over configuration), but I don't want to render it useful for only 50% of the users who want exit codes.

> Perhaps declare in documentation a "reserved" range of return codes that Luigi might define later, the rest being available for application-specific usage.

here it's better to not use a `copy.copy`. In general in Python when handling dictionaries it's enough to use `dict(self._headers)` to create a new shallow copy. Because here your intention is only to add a new field and **never** to alter already assigned fields, a shallow copy using the `dict` keyword is faster and better.

Also the field is "private" so we assume nobody will change the content of the shallow copy other than in this function.

```python
a = {'foo': 42, 'bar': 'fish'}

In [2]: %timeit copy.copy(a)
1000000 loops, best of 3: 695 ns per loop
In [3]: %timeit dict(a)
1000000 loops, best of 3: 272 ns per loop
```

I noticed in the test_thin output that I posted, this algorithm actually trims less when not preserving the range than when it does.  I think this "arbitrary" decision is the reason for that.  I think what is happening is that half your error budget is going into trimming a few points off each end, leaving not much more than half the budget left for the main section.  So it seems that this part of the algorithm may actually be counter-productive.

I think maybe adopting the prescription used by the fixed version of the old code would be better, where the error budget for trimming the ends is taken to be thresh times the fraction of the x range that would be removed.  So if you are trimming off 20% of the x range, you get to use 20% of the total error budget.

You said this is the part that makes you nervous. I think this change is okay, and probably for the better long-term.

It _is_ a subtle behavioral change, and could break some esoteric setups, but it's acceptable.
The SDK config parser uses the standard python `ConfigParser`, and reads from `~/.globus.cfg` and `/etc/globus.cfg`. The CLI config obj is from `configobj`, which, because it thinks of config as something which can be flushed back to disk, can't handle multiple files.

So, if someone had something in `/etc/globus.cfg` which they were relying on in the CLI, it would break.
No one normally will, and I'm okay with breaking those who do -- they'll be primarily internal if they even exist.

My goal with this change was to make the tests less brittle and more expressive. Test cases that depend very closely on detailed structure tend to break any time there's a change, even if that change did not actually break functionality. This decreases developer confidence in tests, since they frequently produce false positives. Also, "golden" tests that check for exact matches on large chunks of output encourage developers to copy and paste new output to quickly unbreak a test. You can see here that I still did a little of that by simply reordering the server fields instead of breaking down into individual test cases.

Instead, it's nice to have tests that test that a specific thing is not broken. We care that the `ssl_certificate_key` directive is present, and that it has the value `/etc/nginx/key.pem`, but it doesn't matter where in the server block it occurs. So ideally this test would go one step further and loop through each server block, trying to find one that contains each necessary directive, regardless of order. But I didn't want to go too deep in this one change. :-)

Zero-sized built-in arrays are illegal in C++ (GCC and Clang accept them, but std::is_array is false for them, so they can't easily be supported).

std::array<T, 0> is legal (C++ says so explicitly) and I've started writing some extra code to support them, but I now think they should be disallowed because they're causing all kinds of problems with numpy. `_dtype_from_pep3118` is *non-deterministic* with zero-sized arrays (I'm guessing because offsets are not unique so do not sort consistently). Given `^T{f:d:(0)I:empty:4x}`, it sometimes gives
```
[('d', '<f4'), ('empty', '<u4', (1,)), ('', 'V4')]
```
and sometimes gives
```
{'names':['d','pad0',''], 'formats':['<f4','V4',('<u4', (0,))], 'offsets':[0,4,4], 'itemsize':8}
```
In the latter case, the dtype match sanity check fails, presumably because the padding is now named `pad0`.

Hmm. Be careful with this kind of pattern (`**kwargs` that gets shared across lots of methods, each of which pays attention to only a few). If you typo a parameter name, it will get silently ignored instead of being flagged as an error. The alternative is to explicitly list all the keyword arguments that any of these extractor methods supports, which is very tedious but at least predictable. Of course, that problem isn't specific to this PR, so I guess it's fine for now.
